flashcards.Back: A set of numbers that includes whole numbers, fractions, and decimals. They can all be represented as a ratio of two integers.
A visual tool used to place fractions and decimals in a specific order. It represents the relative size and position of various rational numbers.
A set of positive numbers starting from zero and including all natural numbers. They are used in basic counting and computation problems.
A numerical representation of a part of a whole. It consists of a numerator on top and a denominator on the bottom.
A number that uses a point to separate the whole number part from the fractional part. It is a common way to represent rational values.
The result of multiplying a number by itself once. It is a fundamental concept in both arithmetic and basic algebra.
The inverse operation of squaring a number. It represents the original value that was multiplied by itself to get a specific product.
The numerical product found by multiplying a number by itself three times. It is used in volume calculations for three-dimensional shapes.
The reverse calculation of finding the cube of a number. It identifies the value that was multiplied three times to reach a specific result.
Mathematical statements that have no valid numerical solution. Examples include dividing any number by zero or taking the square root of a negative value.
The bottom part of a fraction that represents the total number of parts in a whole. If it is zero, the entire expression becomes undefined.
The amount of space contained within a two-dimensional boundary. It is measured in square units for shapes like triangles and rectangles.
The total boundary length of a two-dimensional shape. It is calculated by summing the lengths of all individual sides.
Geometric figures that have only length and width. Common examples in the test include triangles, rectangles, and various polygons.
A three-sided polygon that is a key topic in geometry. Its area is calculated by multiplying half the base by the height.
A four-sided polygon with four right angles. The area is found by multiplying the length of the base by the height.
A general term for closed two-dimensional shapes with straight sides. Students must find their side lengths, area, or perimeter during the exam.
Figures formed by combining multiple simpler geometric shapes. Their total area is found by adding the individual areas of each component.
The three-dimensional space occupied by a solid object. It is a critical measurement for rectangular prisms, cylinders, and pyramids.
The sum of the areas of all external faces of a solid. It provides a measure of the total exterior surface of a three-dimensional object.
Objects that possess length, width, and depth. The exam specifically tests knowledge of prisms, cylinders, and right pyramids.
Solid objects with six rectangular faces. Volume is calculated by multiplying length, width, and height together.
Three-dimensional solids where the top and bottom bases are identical and aligned. They are used in volume and surface area calculation problems.
A solid with two parallel circular bases of equal size. It is a common three-dimensional shape used in geometry questions.
A solid with a polygonal base and a vertex directly above the center. Its volume is one-third of the base area times the height.
A geometric rule used to determine unknown side lengths in right triangles. It states that the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of squares of the other sides.
The standard algebraic representation of the Pythagorean theorem. It is used to calculate the hypotenuse or a leg in a right triangle.
A triangle that contains one ninety-degree angle. It is the only type of triangle where the Pythagorean theorem can be applied.
The longest side of a right triangle, located opposite the right angle. Its length is represented by the variable c in the Pythagorean formula.
A major test section involving the use of variables and equations. It requires knowledge of expressions, linear equations, and functional relationships.
Mathematical phrases involving constants and variables of the first degree. They are commonly added, subtracted, or multiplied in algebra.
The process of rewriting a linear expression as a product of its simplest parts. It is used to simplify complex algebraic statements.
A mathematical statement asserting the equality of two expressions. It forms a straight line when graphed on a coordinate plane.
A group of two or more equations that share the same variables. The solution is the point where their graphs intersect.
A technique for solving a system of equations by plotting lines on a grid. The solution is identified as the coordinates of the intersection point.
A way to solve systems of equations by replacing one variable with an equivalent expression. It simplifies the system into a single equation with one variable.
Also known as elimination, this technique solves systems by adding equations together. This step removes one variable to solve for the other.
Equations that include a variable raised to the second power. They typically yield two solutions when solved for a single variable.
A mathematical relationship where each input has exactly one output. They can be represented using tables, graphs, or algebraic rules.
A measure of the steepness and direction of a line. It is calculated as the change in vertical value divided by the change in horizontal value.
The speed at which one variable changes in relation to another. It is synonymous with the slope of a linear function.
The process of arranging fractions and decimals based on their values. This is typically done on a number line to show relative size.
A symbol, usually a letter, used to represent an unknown numerical value in an equation. It is the building block of all algebraic problem solving.
Mathematical statements that compare two expressions using symbols like less than or greater than. They represent a range of possible solutions.
A tool provided to students during the math test. It contains essential formulas for geometry and measurement to eliminate the need for memorization.
A key topic on the math exam focusing on geometric figures. It includes calculating dimensions, area, and volume for various shapes.
A two-dimensional grid used to plot points and graph equations. It consists of a horizontal x-axis and a vertical y-axis.
The two main components of a function. The input is the independent variable, while the output is the dependent result.
The study of patterns and relationships within sets of numbers. It is one of the foundational areas tested in the math section.
The average value of a set of numbers. It is found by summing all values and dividing by the total count of items.
The middle value in a set of numbers when arranged in order. It represents the central point of the data distribution.
